content:
(cc: @Andreas Hindborg @FUJITA Tomonori )
Hey! So I mentioned in one of the RFL meetings that I've been rebasing rvkms and trying to figure out how to handle all of the time related changes, and one of the issues I hit was whether or not to actually be using Instant types in the modesetting vblank API - since timestamps for vblanks usually come from a timing source on the modesetting device itself and not one of the standard system clocks, but the C side of DRM this uses ktime_t for vblank timestamps.
ClockSource on its own though doesn't really seem completely appropriate though, we can't provide a clock ID for a source like this since none exists.
So I had the idea of potentially splitting ClockSource into two traits to deal with this: a TimeSource that any kind of timestamp providing source can implement (-maybe- with ktime_get(), but I'm not sure yet), and then ClockSource which would be a supertrait of TimeSource and handle all of the kernel timer specific bits, e.g. ID. Then we would be using Instant<T: TimeSource> and only binding T to ClockSource as needed. Does this seem reasonable?
It makes sense to me, for example hrtimer would require a ClockSource because these are supported with a hrtimer.
I guess my comment on sealing ClockSource gets resolved in a different way with this TimeSource idea.

pub unsafe trait TimeSource {
    fn now() -> ktime_t
}

but does your TimeSource impl always return non-negative ktime_t?
Because Instant - Instant cannot overflow.
We were talking about just assigning an ID to your custom source. What would prevent this?

Andreas Hindborg said:

We were talking about just assigning an ID to your custom source. What would prevent this?

I'm not sure if anything would prevent it so much as I'm not really sure what the purpose would be? I was under the assumption that clockid_t was only relevant for core kernel clock sources, and something like a custom clock source for vblanks wouldn't necessarily correlate with a kernel timer at all. (-technically- it does correlate for rvkms, but that is the exception rather than the rule.

And having a distinction between non-core kernel time sources and core kernel time sources would let us do things we probably want anyway, like ensuring you can't try scheduling an HrTimer using an Instant<VblankTime> (or whatever we would call it).

Boqun Feng said:

pub unsafe trait TimeSource {
    fn now() -> ktime_t
}

but does your TimeSource impl always return non-negative ktime_t?

Also - I believe it should. a negative vblank timestamp wouldn't make much sense
Do we actually use ClockSource::ID?
Ah, hrtimer_setup needs it. What clockid_t does a driver that uses own clock source pass to hrtimer_setup?
I thought that your driver uses hrtimer but hrtimer needs the proper clockid. The driver needs only Instant and Delta?

So: rvkms specifically uses an hrtimer using monotonic time for emulating vblanks - so it's a bit of an exception and not a typical usecase. display hardware typically has its own vertical blank timer that a driver usually reads from

So the places where we need our own TimeSource are just for returning the vblank timestamp to the DRM subsystem for drivers implementing vblank support. We never would actually want to use that TimeSource for scheduling an hrtimer
The places where uses own TimerSource needs Instant/Delta with the own ktime_get(); does not use hrtimer, right?
The idea to split the ClockSource sounds reasonable.
TImeSource name is a bit confusing though. A clock is a source of time information, generating time values. So ClockSource name makes sense. But Time is a value from the clock source.

Yeah - though one thing to mention is they don't use ktime_get() either, it's really just on the C side of things DRM uses ktime_t for describing vblank timestamps - so ideally we want to be able to return an Instant type in vblank callbacks. We wouldn't be able to use the ktime_get() interface as-is anyhow because we need device context to access vblank timestamp registers
The name ktime_get()for the trait method might be a bad idea ;-) It should be called now(), right? ;-)

Lyude said:

Yeah - though one thing to mention is they don't use ktime_get() either, it's really just on the C side of things DRM uses ktime_t for describing vblank timestamps - so ideally we want to be able to return an Instant type in vblank callbacks. We wouldn't be able to use the ktime_get() interface as-is anyhow because we need device context to access vblank timestamp registers

you mean you won't call the ktime_get() C function, right? Surely you can implement a now() function for your TimeSource?

Boqun Feng said:

The name ktime_get()for the trait method might be a bad idea ;-) It should be called now(), right? ;-)

Yeah, we should use now() or current_time() or something instead of ktime_get().

pub unsafe trait Foo {
    fn now() -> ktime_t
}

IMHO, this trait implements a mechanism generating current time values, which is the definition of clock source. So I think that we should use ClockSource for this trait. We need a different name for clockid_t.
Also on the naming point - what about ClockSource and KernelClock or KernelClockSource?
My preference are ClockSource and ClockSourceId
or ClockSrouce and ClockId
because ClockId trait gives a valid clockid_t
oh yeah that works

Honestly while thinking about this after a bit - while I don't think we're really supposed to return negative timestamps, I'm not really sure that we gain much using Instant here - especially since it would require us to make sure that the timestamp that we want to return isn't < 0. So it might actually make sense to just use Delta here, and also add something so that we can convert Instant to Delta specifically for handling rvkms's case of emulating vblanks using an hrtimer
You don't need to make Instant non-negative actually, you just need to make sure Instant - Instant won't overflow.
Oh. Though, that's still a check that we need the driver to do and I'm not totally sure it matters much to the vblank core.

With other clock sources, an instant is in the range [0,i32::MAX], which means that the difference can be anywhere in [-i32::MAX,i32::MAX], i.e. it's the largest range so that differences don't overflow.
(I think ktime_t is an i32?)
no it's i64
then i32 -> i64 in what I said
But it means that there's no need for overflow checks when subtracting instants - with the range they're in, it can never overflow.
But it means that it does need to be non-negative.

BTW on that note - would we be open to having some simple arithmatic for adding/subtracting Deltas from Instant? One issue I did definitely notice is that without this, it's pretty difficult to work with Instants in regards to scheduling a timer at a precise time in the future
If we don't have methods for adding a duration to an instant, we should.
It should probably force an overflow check, though.

Yeah - I was thinking that with this sort of thing maybe we should just handle it the same way that rust handles arithmatic in general - panicking in situations where wrapping/saturating isn't explicitly specified and an over/underflow occurred
Instant + Delta should be supported
Delta + Delta as well.
We need Instant - Delta as well, btw. And Delta % Delta (this one sounds silly but it is actually useful for figuring out where in a repeating interval of time we are from a given time
Yes Instant - Delta is fine.
Delta itself is just a normal i64
but we probably want to avoid hardware division implementation (software division is fine)

Boqun Feng said:

but we probably want to avoid hardware division implementation (software division is fine)

What do you mean by this btw?

Lyude said:

What do you mean by this btw?

When you implement Delta % Delta, you need to check the generated code, and it cannot use division instruction on the architectures that don't support them at the kernel context.
oh dear
I never realized there were architectures that didn't let you divide in kernel context
do_div() exists for a purpose.
I might not be the expert for this topic, you need to consult linux-arch for this.

this really feels like something that the compiler should be handling and not the developer. Like, if I were bringing up an architecture it feels like it would be easy to spot these things. But I also am not an expert on ISAs, and I'm not really sure if I've got the time to put in for that even though I really do need to be able to do modulos here
@Boqun Feng is "you can't do a % b` actually documented anywhere btw? I feel like I've seen code doing modulos in the kernel before

otherwise, tbh, i'm kind of just tempted to say we should ask upstream rust about this or see if this sort of thing can be handled (by someone else) as op implementations on i8/16/32/64/etc. otherwise i worry this code is never getting upstream :S
I did say "consult", didn't I? ;-)
So you're not going to face this alone.
For 32bit architectures, I think you will get a link error when doing 64bit division.
FYI: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72ke45eOwckMhWHvmwxc03dxr4rnxxKvx+HvWdBLopZfrQ@mail.gmail.com/
so I was not accurate, it is “i64 / i64” that is not fully supported for all the architectures

honestly it's just that the change I made with rvkms that brought me down this rabbit hole was not supposed to be that difficult and i'm trying quite hard not to pile additional work on top of what I need to already get upstream.
is there a way i can just prevent this driver from being built on architectures that don't deal with this?
Make “Delta / Delta” only exists for CONFIG_64BIT, and make your driver depends on CONFIG_64BIT
looks like i'm fine if I just block this on 64 bit architectures, so i'll implement the other ops and just leave delta out of this and convert to/from nanos
sorry, 32 bit arches
I think making “Delta / Delta” depend on CONFIG_64BIT is the way to go, and if one accidentally do that on 32 bit kernel, compile can tell them.
sgtm! thank you btw ♥\
